lessons.title: Annotation Basics
lessons.summary: Learn the concept and role of annotations. Understand built-in annotations such as @Override, @Deprecated, @SuppressWarnings, and @FunctionalInterface, as well as meta-annotations like @Target and @Retention.

contents.title: What Are Annotations?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The role of **annotations** (attaching metadata)
- Standard [[annotations]] such as @Override, @Deprecated, and @SuppressWarnings
- Where annotations are placed and their effects

Understand the mechanism for "adding information to code."

contents.title: Built-in Annotations
contents.body:
| Annotation | Description |
|---|---|
| @Override | Indicates that a method overrides a superclass method. Causes a compilation error if it does not actually override. |
| @Deprecated | Indicates that an element is deprecated. Using it produces a compile-time warning. |
| @SuppressWarnings | Suppresses compiler warnings. Specify warning categories such as "unchecked", "deprecation", and "rawtypes". |
| @FunctionalInterface | Indicates that an interface is a functional interface (has exactly one abstract method). |
| @SafeVarargs | Suppresses warnings about heap pollution from varargs methods. |
contents.note: @Deprecated(forRemoval = true) is available since Java 9 and indicates that the element is scheduled for removal in a future version.

contents.title: Meta-annotations
contents.body:
**Meta-annotations** are annotations applied to annotation definitions.
| Annotation | Description |
|---|---|
| @Target | Specifies the kinds of elements to which the annotation can be applied (ElementType enum). |
| @Retention | Specifies the retention scope of the annotation information (RetentionPolicy enum). |
| @Documented | Specifies that the annotation information should be included in Javadoc. |
| @Inherited | Specifies that the annotation is inherited by subclasses. |
| @Repeatable | Specifies that an annotation can be applied multiple times to the same element (Java 8+). |
contents.note: The default @Retention if not specified is RetentionPolicy.CLASS. RUNTIME is required to access it via **reflection**.

contents.title: ElementType and RetentionPolicy
contents.body:
ElementType is an enum that restricts where an **annotation** can be applied.
To specify multiple ElementTypes, use array notation: @Target({ElementType.TYPE, ElementType.METHOD}).
RetentionPolicy has three levels — SOURCE has the shortest and RUNTIME has the longest retention period.
| Policy | Description |
|---|---|
| SOURCE | Used by the compiler and not included in the class file (e.g., @Override). |
| CLASS | Included in the class file but cannot be accessed at runtime via reflection. |
| RUNTIME | Accessible at runtime via reflection (widely used in frameworks). |
contents.note: @Override has @Retention(SOURCE), so it is not included in the [[compiled]] [[class]] file.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Annotation Basics
contents.body:
| Annotation | Description |
|---|---|
| @Override | Applying it to a method that does not actually override causes a compilation error. |
| @FunctionalInterface | Applying it to an interface with more than one abstract method causes a compilation error. |
| @SuppressWarnings values | Specified as strings. For multiple values: @SuppressWarnings({"unchecked", "deprecation"}). |
| Default @Retention | Remember that it is CLASS. |
Omitting @Target allows the annotation to be applied to nearly all elements.

quizzes.question: Which statement about the @Override annotation is correct?
quizzes.explanation: @Override is an annotation that explicitly indicates a method overrides a superclass method. Applying it to a method that does not actually override causes a compilation error. This helps detect issues like method name typos early.

choices.text: Applying it to a non-overriding method causes a compilation error
Without it, the override becomes invalid
It can also be applied to fields
It can be retrieved at runtime via reflection

quizzes.question: What is the default retention policy when @Retention is not specified?
quizzes.explanation: When @Retention is not specified, the default is RetentionPolicy.CLASS. In this case, the annotation information is included in the class file but cannot be retrieved at runtime via reflection. Specifying RUNTIME makes it accessible at runtime as well.

choices.text: There is no default; it must always be specified

quizzes.question: To restrict an annotation's target to methods only, specify @Target(ElementType.___).
quizzes.explanation: ElementType.METHOD restricts the annotation target to methods only. Specifying @Target(ElementType.METHOD) causes a compilation error when the annotation is applied to classes or fields.

lessons.title: Custom Annotations
lessons.summary: Learn how to define custom annotations. Understand the @interface syntax, default values for elements, configuring @Retention and @Target, and marker annotations and single-value annotations.

contents.title: Custom Annotations Overview
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Defining **custom annotations** with @interface
- Declaring elements (attributes) and their default values
- **Meta-annotations** such as @Retention and @Target

You will learn how to design your own [[annotations]].

contents.title: Defining Annotations with @interface Syntax
contents.body:
**Annotations** are defined with the @interface keyword (not interface).
Elements are declared in the form of [[methods]] with no arguments and no implementation body.
Default values can be specified with the default keyword.
An annotation with no elements is called a marker annotation.
If there is a single element named value(), the element name can be omitted when using the annotation (single-value annotation).
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Rules for Annotation Elements
contents.body:
The return types of **annotation** elements are limited to the following.
[[Primitive types]] (int, double, boolean, etc.), [[String]], Class<?>, enum types, and annotation types.
As well as one-dimensional [[arrays]] of these types.
Collection types like [[ArrayList]] and Map cannot be used.
Default values must be [[compile]]-time constants.
null cannot be used as a default value.
Java reserved words cannot be used as element names.
contents.note: Specifying null as a default value for an **annotation** element causes a compilation error. Use an empty string "" or empty array {} instead.

contents.title: Configuring @Retention and @Target
contents.body:
**Custom annotations** typically include @Retention and @Target.
@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME) is required for reading via **reflection**.
Using @Target to restrict applicable targets prevents unintended usage.
To specify multiple applicable targets, use array notation.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Custom Annotations
contents.body:
@interface is a different keyword from interface. Do not confuse them.
An element named value() allows omitting the element name and writing just the value when using the annotation.
However, if there are other elements without default values besides value(), the name cannot be omitted.
null cannot be used for **annotation** elements. It also cannot be used as a default value.
Curly braces {} can be omitted for single-element arrays.

quizzes.question: Which type cannot be used as a custom annotation element?
quizzes.explanation: Annotation element types are limited to primitive types, String, Class<?>, enum types, annotation types, and one-dimensional arrays of these types. ArrayList<String> is a collection type and cannot be used as an annotation element type.

quizzes.question: To define a custom annotation, use the ___ keyword.
quizzes.explanation: Custom annotations are defined using the @interface keyword. Note that it differs from the regular interface keyword by having an @ prefix. Example: @interface MyAnnotation { String value(); }

quizzes.question: The following annotation definition has an error. Which part is problematic?
quizzes.explanation: Specifying null as a default value for an annotation element causes a compilation error. null cannot be used as a value for annotation elements. An empty string "" or similar should be used instead.

choices.text: null is specified as the default value
name() does not have a default specified
The default value type for version() is invalid

quizzes.question: Complete the code to define a custom annotation.
quizzes.explanation: @Retention specifies the annotation's retention period, and @Target specifies the applicable target. Specifying RUNTIME allows access via reflection.

quizzes.question: Select all Java standard annotations.
quizzes.explanation: @Override, @Deprecated, @SuppressWarnings, and @FunctionalInterface are Java standard annotations. @Autowired is a Spring Framework annotation.

lessons.title: Using Annotations in Practice
lessons.summary: Learn @Repeatable annotations, retrieving annotation information via reflection, and examples of annotation usage in major frameworks.

contents.title: Practical Annotation Usage Overview
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Retrieving **annotation** information via **reflection**
- @Repeatable (repeating [[annotations]]) Java 8+
- Examples of annotation usage in frameworks

You will learn how annotations are used in real-world scenarios.

contents.title: @Repeatable Annotations
contents.note: The container **annotation**'s @Retention must be the same or a longer retention period than the repeatable annotation.

contents.title: Retrieving Annotations via Reflection
contents.body:
You can retrieve **annotation** information at runtime using the **reflection** API.
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| getAnnotation(Class) | Returns the annotation of the specified type, or null if not present. |
| getAnnotations() | Returns all annotations as an array. |
| isAnnotationPresent(Class) | Returns a boolean indicating whether the annotation is present. |
Only annotations with @Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME) can be retrieved.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Reflection with @Repeatable
contents.body:
There are two ways to retrieve @Repeatable **annotations** via **reflection**.
getAnnotationsByType(Class) retrieves @Repeatable annotations directly as an array.
You can also retrieve the container annotation with getAnnotation(Class).
If @Repeatable is applied only once, no container annotation is generated.
If applied two or more times, the annotations are stored in the container annotation.
contents.note: getAnnotationsByType() was added in Java 8 and can be used regardless of whether @Repeatable is present.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Using Annotations in Practice
contents.body:
@Repeatable requires a container **annotation** definition, whose value() must be an array type of the repeatable annotation.
To retrieve annotations via **reflection**, @Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME) is required.
getAnnotation() returns a single annotation of the specified type, or null if not present.
getAnnotationsByType() retrieves @Repeatable annotations as an array.
Be familiar with annotation usage in frameworks (@Test, @Entity, @Autowired, etc.) as concepts.

quizzes.question: What is required when defining a @Repeatable annotation?
quizzes.explanation: To define a @Repeatable annotation, a container annotation that stores the repeatable annotations is required. The container annotation must have a value() element whose type is an array of the repeatable annotation (e.g., Role[] value()).

choices.text: A container annotation with an array of the repeatable annotation

quizzes.question: What @Retention value is required to retrieve annotation information via reflection?
quizzes.explanation: To retrieve annotation information at runtime via reflection, @Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME) is required. SOURCE is discarded after compilation, and CLASS is included in the class file but cannot be retrieved at runtime via reflection.

choices.text: Any value allows retrieval via reflection

quizzes.question: What is the output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: The urgent() method has @Priority(1), so value() returns 1. The normal() method has @Priority without a specified value, so the default value of 5 is used. Therefore, the output is "1,5".
